---
title: "Counting Fibonacci primes: what a finite computation can and cannot add"
description: "Treating the 57 known Fibonacci prime indices as a dataset. A density heuristic, a coverage-validated prediction interval, a null-model battery that finds no structure, and an exact divisibility layer."
author: "Madhava Gaikwad"
date: "2026-06-07"
categories: [number-theory, primes, statistics, reproducibility]
---

A Fibonacci prime is a Fibonacci number $F_n$ that is prime. As of June 2025 there
are 57 known indices $n$ for which $F_n$ is prime or probably prime. They range
from $F_3$ up to $F_{11964299}$. The largest one proven prime is $F_{201107}$. The
rest are probable primes. They pass a Miller-Rabin test and have not been
certified. Whether there are infinitely many Fibonacci primes is open. No pattern
is known beyond a density heuristic.

This study treats the 57 indices as a dataset. It asks whether any visible
structure survives scrutiny, and where such structure lives. The mathematics
behind the question is already established in the literature. The contribution is
a method. It adds an uncertainty-quantified prediction layer, a null-model battery
that separates real structure from sampling noise, and a fully cited
reproduction. All computations run from released Python scripts at
[github.com/krimler/k-bonacci](https://github.com/krimler/k-bonacci).

## The density heuristic

Start with the count. A random integer of the size of $F_p \sim \varphi^p/\sqrt5$
is prime with probability about $1/\log F_p \approx 1/(p\log\varphi)$. The
Fibonacci numbers form a divisibility sequence, so $F_m \mid F_n$ exactly when
$m \mid n$ for $m > 2$. A prime $F_p$ with $p > 4$ therefore forces $p$ prime.
Summing the prime probability over primes and applying Mertens' theorem injects a
factor $e^{\gamma}$, where $\gamma$ is the Euler-Mascheroni constant. Grantham and
Granville conjecture [@grantham2024fibonacci]

$$
\#\{\, n \le N : F_n \text{ prime} \,\} \;\sim\; e^{\gamma}\,\log_{\varphi} N.
$$ {#eq-count}

At $N = 1.2\times10^{7}$ the heuristic predicts about 60.3 indices. The observed
count is 57.

A naive maximum-likelihood estimate of the leading constant sits about 19% above
$e^{\gamma}$. The study reconciles this. Fitting the constant over the Fibonacci
indices alone gives an estimate within 3% of $e^{\gamma}$. A pooled estimate over
four related division-type families gives $2.11$ with a 95% interval of
$[1.75, 2.48]$, which contains $e^{\gamma}\approx 1.781$. The apparent excess
decomposes into three parts. There is the choice of $\log X$ versus $\log X + E_1$
in the Mertens partial sum. There is small-sample noise. There are family-specific
divisor factors. The data cannot separate $e^{\gamma}\approx 1.781$ from
$\sqrt{\pi}\approx 1.772$ at one standard deviation. Resolving the two would need
about $4\times10^{4}$ primes, reachable only by pooling more families.

## Prediction with quantified uncertainty

The indices grow geometrically. Define the gaps $g_k = \log n_{k+1} - \log n_k$.
To the resolution of 57 points these gaps are independent and exponentially
distributed. A Kolmogorov-Smirnov test against the exponential gives $p = 0.63$.
The lag-1 autocorrelation is $-0.09$ with $p = 0.53$. The gaps behave like a
memoryless Poisson process in $\log n$.

The reference literature performs no interval estimation. This study adds one.
Treating the next index as $n_{37}\cdot e^{g}$ with $g$ exponential yields a 90%
predictive window of $[2.04, 5.07]\times10^{5}$. The true next index is 397379,
which lies inside. A one-step-ahead backtest over the 46 predictable indices puts
40 of them inside their nominal 90% intervals. That is 87% coverage, which is well
calibrated for a one-parameter model.

## Residue statistics

The study probes the indices for structure. It runs residue-class biases modulo 4,
5, and 10, a Lemke Oliver-Soundararajan consecutive-residue correlation, a Sophie
Germain depletion, an order-of-primeness enrichment, and a Benford leading-digit
test. All return null. As a global check the authors simulated 3000 index sets
from the $e^{\gamma}$ density model and compared nine statistics. The observed data
was an outlier in zero of the nine.

The conclusion is stated plainly. No structure is detectable at $N = 57$. Against
percent-level biases measured on millions of primes, the power of 57 points is
negligible. The Lemke Oliver-Soundararajan effect cannot apply here. It is a
short-range effect among consecutive primes. The positions studied here are
long-range, with a median gap near 5000.

## The divisibility layer

The location of the indices carries no algebraic handle. The factor structure
does. Here the established cases are theorems, and the study adds one conjectural
set.

For Fibonacci numbers the rank of apparition $z(p)$ is the least $m$ with
$p \mid F_m$. Lagarias proved that the primes dividing the Lucas numbers have
density $2/3$ [@lagarias1985lucas]. The study confirms $P(z(p)\text{ even}) = 2/3$
numerically, measuring $0.66684$ over 148931 primes. The Pisano multiplier and the
2-adic valuation of $z(p)$ both follow from Cubre and Rouse's proof via a
Chebotarev argument [@cubre2014divisibility].

For $k$-bonacci sequences with $k \ge 3$ the divisibility-sequence property fails.
The governing object is the Galois group of the characteristic polynomial
$x^{k} - x^{k-1} - \cdots - 1$, which is the full symmetric group $S_k$ for $k$
even or prime. The prime-splitting types, and so the period of the sequence modulo
$p$, follow the cycle-type densities of $S_k$ by Chebotarev. The study confirms the
$S_3$ and $S_4$ densities numerically.

For the Tribonacci period $h(p)$ the study records a conjectural density. Over 5131
primes below $5\times10^{4}$ it estimates $P(h(p)\text{ odd}) \approx 0.397$. The
exact constant is open. The study corrects an earlier draft that called the
Tribonacci divisor and entry-point densities computable for any order. The general
case is open. Järviniemi's positive lower density under GRH is the current state of
the art [@jarviniemi2022density].

## What is known versus what is added

The value of this study is the line it draws. The count law, the $e^{\gamma}$
constant, the $2/3$ Lucas density, the $S_k$ splitting densities, and the
Tribonacci period structure are established theorems with citations. The additions
are three. There is a coverage-validated prediction interval for the index counts.
There is a reusable null-model battery for separating structure from sampling
noise. There is a fully cited, script-based reproduction.

## Reproducibility

All results come from self-contained Python scripts using NumPy, SciPy, and
Matplotlib. The index data are the OEIS b-files for the Fibonacci, Lucas, Mersenne,
and Wagstaff sequences. The code is at
[github.com/krimler/k-bonacci](https://github.com/krimler/k-bonacci).

## Read the paper

The full paper is here: [Fibonacci and k-bonacci prime statistics (PDF and details)](../../papers/fibonacci-primes.qmd).

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
